lessons.title: std::flat_map and std::flat_set: Array-Backed Associative Containers
lessons.motivation: std::map and std::set are C++ workhorses, but under the hood they use a red-black tree: every node is a separate heap allocation, and traversing to the next element means chasing a pointer. On read-heavy workloads — lookups and iteration — this causes constant cache misses. C++23 introduced std::flat_map and std::flat_set: containers with the same interface but storing data in sorted vectors. Binary search over a vector is far more cache-friendly than a tree, and for the typical use case of "fill once, query many times" this delivers a measurable speedup without changing calling code.
lessons.theory:
std::flat_map<K, V> stores keys and values in two separate sorted vectors. Lookup is performed by binary search over the keys vector in O(log n), just like std::map, but accessing consecutive keys walks contiguous memory — the CPU cache is used efficiently. Header: <flat_map>. std::flat_set<T> similarly stores elements in a single sorted vector; header: <flat_set>.

The interface of flat_map and flat_set deliberately mirrors map/set: operator[], at(), find(), contains(), insert(), erase(), and range-for over key-value pairs. Switching from map to flat_map often amounts to changing one type name. One important difference concerns iterators: dereferencing a flat_map iterator returns a proxy pair of references (std::pair<const K&, V&>), which is a temporary object — structured bindings must be declared as auto [k, v], not auto& [k, v].

Performance depends on the access pattern. flat_map wins for reads, iteration, and small-to-medium datasets (thousands to tens of thousands of elements). It loses when elements are inserted frequently in the middle: each insertion requires shifting elements in O(n). If the input data is already sorted, the std::sorted_unique tag lets you construct the container without an extra sort pass. Decision rule: build the table once and only read it afterward — prefer flat_map; insert and erase frequently at arbitrary positions — use regular map.

flat_map exposes its underlying storage through keys() and values(), which return const references to the internal vectors. This allows passing the key list to an algorithm without copying, enables SIMD-friendly sequential access, or simply retrieves the size without iterating. The default backing container is std::vector, but any random-access container (e.g. std::deque) can be substituted via template parameters.

examples.title: Basic flat_map: same interface as map
examples.expectedOutput:
Alice: 98
Bob: 87
Carol: 92
contains Dave: false
examples.explanation: flat_map behaves exactly like map: the duplicate assignment scores["Alice"] = 98 updates the value, iteration visits elements in sorted key order, and contains() uses binary search. Notice auto [name, score] without &: a flat_map iterator returns a proxy pair of temporary references, and binding by lvalue reference does not compile.

examples.title: flat_set and the sorted_unique tag
examples.explanation: flat_set keeps elements in a sorted vector and silently drops duplicates on insert. The std::sorted_unique tag signals to the flat_map constructor that the input is already ordered and unique — the container skips the O(n log n) sort and copies the data directly. Only use this tag when you can guarantee order and uniqueness: violating either is undefined behavior.

examples.title: Configuration table: find() and keys()
examples.expectedOutput:
port = 8080
retries = 3
missing not found
key count: 5
examples.explanation: flat_map is an excellent fit for configuration tables: built once at startup, then used exclusively for reads. find() performs binary search and returns an iterator or end(). keys() gives direct access to the internal keys vector without copying — handy for passing to algorithms or logging. Similarly, values() exposes the values vector.

lessons.title: std::move_only_function and std::spanstream
lessons.motivation: std::function<Sig> is a convenient wrapper for storing any callable, but it has a hidden constraint: the stored callable must be copyable. This blocks capturing a unique_ptr, std::promise, or any other move-only resource in a lambda you want to store in std::function. C++23 solves this with std::move_only_function<Sig>, which lifts the copyability requirement and accepts any movable callable. The second utility in this lesson is std::spanstream: std::stringstream allocates a buffer on the heap at construction, which is unacceptable in embedded code that forbids allocations or in real-time handlers. std::ospanstream and std::ispanstream operate over a fixed external buffer with zero heap activity.
lessons.theory:
std::move_only_function<Sig> is declared in the <functional> header and behaves like std::function, but is a move-only type: it can be moved but not copied. This makes it possible to store lambdas that capture std::unique_ptr, std::promise, file descriptors, or any other resource for which copying is not defined. The call syntax is unchanged: f(args...). Two methods specific to std::function — .target() and .target_type() — are absent in move_only_function, which slightly simplifies the implementation and yields marginally better performance.

Practical use cases include task queues (each task exclusively owns a resource), callback chains (promise/future), and processing pipelines where each stage holds unique ownership over its data. Attempting to copy a move_only_function is a compile error — an explicit, statically checked contract rather than silent undefined behavior or a runtime exception.

std::spanstream is a family of stream types from the <spanstream> header that operate over external memory. std::ospanstream takes a std::span<char> and writes data into that buffer via operator<<, allocating no heap memory. After writing, the .span() method returns a span bounded to the bytes actually written. std::ispanstream takes a std::span<const char> and parses it with operator>> exactly like std::istringstream would. Both classes inherit from std::basic_spanstream, which supports combined read-write access when needed.

The key value of spanstream is predictable memory usage: the buffer lives exactly as long as the caller decides, with no hidden heap traffic. This matters in real-time systems, embedded platforms with restricted heaps, or high-throughput servers where the allocator becomes a point of contention. One limitation: ospanstream cannot grow — if more data than the buffer size is written, the output is silently truncated and the stream enters an error state, which must be checked via os.good() or os.tellp().

examples.title: move_only_function: storing a lambda that captures unique_ptr
examples.explanation: The lambda captures a unique_ptr via std::move, making the lambda itself move-only. std::function would refuse to store such a callable because copying it is impossible. std::move_only_function accepts the move without complaint: each task in the vector holds exclusive ownership of its resource.

examples.title: move_only_function as a parameter and transferring ownership
examples.explanation: The run() parameter accepts move_only_function by value, transferring ownership of the callable along with all its captured resources. The second block shows an explicit move: after std::move(f), f is in a valid-but-unspecified state and g fully owns the stored function. Trying to copy f instead of moving it would be a compile error.

examples.title: ospanstream and ispanstream: streaming I/O without heap allocation
examples.explanation: ospanstream writes the text representation of values directly into the stack array buf. After writing, os.span() returns a span bounded to the bytes actually written — not the entire buffer. ispanstream parses the buffer exactly as istringstream would parse a string, but without any allocation. Note the explicit std::span<const char>: the ispanstream constructor takes a span, not a pointer, and there is no implicit conversion from a braced-init list.

lessons.title: std::stacktrace: Standard Library Stack Tracing
lessons.motivation: Before C++23, getting a stack trace in portable C++ code was impossible without platform-specific APIs: backtrace() + backtrace_symbols() on POSIX, CaptureStackBackTrace() + SymFromAddr() on Windows, or third-party libraries such as boost::stacktrace or libunwind. C++23 introduced std::stacktrace — a standard, portable way to capture the current call stack at any point in a program. You can now attach a stack trace to exceptions, log it on unexpected state, or print it on a crash, without a single platform-dependent line of code.
lessons.theory:
std::stacktrace::current() captures the call stack at the point of call and returns a std::stacktrace object — a range of std::stacktrace_entry frames. Each frame describes one level of the stack: .description() returns the function name (may be mangled or "??" without debug symbols), .source_file() gives the source file path, and .source_line() gives the line number. std::stacktrace supports range-for, indexing, size(), empty(), to_string(), and formatting via std::format/std::println. On GCC, the linker flag -lstdc++_libbacktrace is required; without it the program compiles but fails to link for execution.

The quality of data in a stacktrace depends directly on the availability of debug symbols. In a debug build (-g) you will see full function names and accurate line numbers. In a release build with optimization (-O2 without -g) most frames show "??" instead of names — the compiler has inlined functions, reordered code, and discarded symbol information. This is not a defect in the standard; it reflects the true state of the executable. Debug information must be built in for the trace to be human-readable.

std::stacktrace::current() accepts two optional arguments: skip (how many top frames to omit) and max_depth (the maximum number of frames to capture). This matters in wrapper functions and exception constructors: passing skip=1 removes the constructor itself from the trace and starts from the calling code. Limiting depth reduces capture cost — calling std::stacktrace::current() is not free and is not intended for hot paths.

The canonical use of std::stacktrace is enriched exceptions. A custom error class stores a std::stacktrace field filled during construction, and the exception handler prints it alongside the error message. This dramatically reduces time-to-diagnosis compared to having only the exception text. std::stacktrace integrates fully with the C++23 formatting system: std::println("{}", trace) prints the entire trace, and std::println("{}", trace[i]) prints a single frame.

examples.title: std::stacktrace API: capture and basic operations
examples.expectedOutput:
empty.size(): 0
empty.empty(): true
has frames: true
repr non-null: true
copy equals original: true
examples.explanation: std::stacktrace is a full value type: it can be copied, compared, and formatted. A default-constructed instance has size() == 0. current() always returns a non-empty stacktrace because at least one frame (main or its runtime wrapper) is always present. Note that the concrete function names and file paths inside frames depend on debug symbol availability and may show "??" in optimized builds. Note on Compiler Explorer: std::stacktrace cannot be linked for execution in the sandbox because libstdc++_libbacktrace is not available there; both examples were verified to compile with exit code 0, and the expected output was derived analytically.

examples.title: Exception class with an embedded stack trace
examples.expectedOutput:
caught: negative value not allowed
has trace: true
diagnosis available: true
examples.explanation: DiagnosticError captures the stack trace inside its constructor with skip=1 to exclude the constructor itself — the caller's frames are what matters, not the internals of DiagnosticError. In the catch block, e.what() always returns the message passed at construction, and e.trace.empty() is always false: the frames for process, risky_operation, and main are guaranteed to be present. In a debug build, iterating over e.trace would print full function names and line numbers; in a release build, "??" or hex addresses. This is the canonical production pattern for std::stacktrace.

lessons.title: C++23 Language Utilities: contains, resize_and_overwrite, invoke_r, is_scoped_enum
lessons.motivation: Some of the most useful C++23 additions are small, targeted improvements that fix long-standing inconveniences. std::string::contains() eliminates the awkward s.find(sub) != std::string::npos idiom. resize_and_overwrite() lets you fill a string via a C API without zero-initializing memory you are about to overwrite anyway. std::invoke_r<R> adds explicit return-type control where std::invoke left it to type deduction. And std::is_scoped_enum replaces an unreadable combination of is_enum_v && !is_convertible_v with a single, self-documenting name. Together they make everyday code cleaner and safer.
lessons.theory:
std::string::contains() (P1679) is the logical companion to starts_with() and ends_with() introduced in C++20. The method has three overloads: std::string_view, char, and const char*. It is available on both std::string and std::string_view. Before C++23, checking for a substring required s.find(sub) != std::string::npos — a verbose expression that is easy to get wrong (forget the != npos part, or confuse npos with size_type). contains() reads like natural English and has pure boolean semantics with no side effects.

std::string::resize_and_overwrite() (P1072) solves the problem of efficiently filling a string via an external API. The ordinary resize(N) zero-initializes all N characters before the caller writes into them — wasted work. resize_and_overwrite(N, op) allocates a buffer of N characters, then calls op(char* p, size_t n), which writes data into the buffer and returns the actual number of characters written. That count becomes the new string length. No zero-initialization takes place. The canonical use case is wrapping snprintf or any C API that writes into a caller-provided buffer.

std::invoke_r<R>(f, args...) (P2136) extends std::invoke with an explicit return type. The callable's return value is converted to R. If R is void, the return value is silently discarded. This is useful in generic code where the result type must be determined by the interface signature rather than the concrete callable's type — for example, in a dispatch table of heterogeneous handlers or when passing a lambda to a typed adapter. Without invoke_r you had to write static_cast<R>(std::invoke(f, args...)), which does not work when R is void.

std::is_scoped_enum<T> and std::is_scoped_enum_v<T> (P1048) is a type trait that distinguishes scoped enumerations (enum class / enum struct) from unscoped ones. Before C++23 the check looked like std::is_enum_v<T> && !std::is_convertible_v<T, std::underlying_type_t<T>> — an expression that requires intimate knowledge of the type system and is nearly unreadable at a glance. The new trait is a single name with obvious semantics. It returns false for non-enum types including int, and true only for enum class.

examples.title: contains and is_scoped_enum
examples.expectedOutput:
contains "C++23": true
contains "C++26": false
contains '2': true
Color is scoped enum: true
Direction is scoped enum: false
int is scoped enum: false
examples.explanation: contains() accepts std::string_view (which covers string literals and std::string) or char — three overloads, none requiring npos arithmetic. std::is_scoped_enum_v<Color> is true because Color is declared as enum class and has no implicit conversion to an integer. Direction is a plain unscoped enum that implicitly converts to int, so the trait returns false. For int the trait is also false: is_scoped_enum only holds for enum types.

examples.title: resize_and_overwrite and invoke_r
examples.expectedOutput:
msg: Value: 42, Pi: 3.14
msg.size(): 19
invoke_r<double>(add, 10, 3): 13
invoke_r<void> works: true
examples.explanation: resize_and_overwrite allocates a 64-character buffer without zero-initialization and hands it to the lambda. snprintf writes the formatted string and returns the number of characters that would have been written without truncation (19 here). The string is given exactly that length, so msg.size() == 19. invoke_r<double> calls add(10, 3) == 13 and converts the result to double, giving 13.0. invoke_r<void> calls the callable for its side effect and completely discards the return value — something that cannot be expressed via a plain static_cast when R is void.
